# 46: Back to four
"Zen-chan, I can handle the food—"
"You just woke up. Let Zen-san handle it and keep your ass in that seat."
"Huh? I don't recall taking orders from a guy who hasn't lifted a finger to help."

Null and Rico’s bickering drew a faint, weary smile from Zentaro as he continued prep and plating in silence. Ena started to reach for a fragment of an old memory but shook her head, turning her focus back to Null.

"So, how was it out there?"
"Shitty. Eir is accelerating the purge of this discarded sector like it's brushing off a mosquito. I wanted to hack the process, but the encryption keys are rotating every few seconds. There's no way to keep up."
"If we can't sneak in through the back door, a frontal assault is suicide."

Touma tipped the bottle back, letting out a short, sharp breath as the liquid scorched his throat.

"That's why we gotta change the target. Zen-san and I talked it over. Heh. Go ahead, tell 'em."

"Bottom line is, Eir already knows your sound. So, we might as well stop hiding and broadcast it across the entire City at once. We'll slam it directly into the brains of those people who've grown too used to their emotional castration. We're gonna make the whole goddamn city glitch."

"Whoa, hold up. That's just straight-up terrorism. Dad, did you hit your head or something?"

"There's no other way to rattle Eir. Ena, Touma, Rico—and Null, you too. You're going back to being a four-piece. You play, and I'll handle the city-wide distribution."

"Back to four?"
.
.
.

# 45 Nauseating Success
"My bad. Maybe you were right after all. There's no way this piece of trash is the key to the final protocol. Put her down." 
"Got it."

Touma's right hand was already drifting toward the holster at his waist. Right then, the heavy soundproof door swung open with a mechanical thud, letting a draft of dry air from the hallway rush into the room. Null, fresh off his mission outside, scanned the room with a look of pure exasperation. Beside him stood Zentaro Makabe.

"Tch. Room reeks like a fucking dumpster, but looks like you actually managed the reboot. God, it's foul in here. And you, blondie—put that heater away for a second."
"You both did well. And Rico, I'm glad to see you've finally woken up."

Zentaro's voice was too clear, too normal, cutting through the stagnant air of the basement like a blade. Touma let go of the holster, though his eyes remained fixed on Rico, who was still slumped on the floor in a daze.

"Were you watching, Dad?"
"No, we just got back. But this disaster and the vital logs tell me enough. Good morning, Rico. How’s the internal state?"

Zentaro walked over and knelt in front of Rico. She wiped the mess from her mouth with the back of her hand and glared back at him with unfocused eyes.

"My head is still full of shitty noise. Those hacks played so bad I’m feeling fucking homicidal."
"The hell did you just say? You got some goddamn nerve, I'll give you that."
"But she's alive. More alive than we could have hoped for. Sorry for the trouble. You have my thanks."

Zentaro took Rico's pulse, checking her vitals manually, and gave a satisfied nod.
.
.
.

# 44 Gutter-Tier Ecstasy
The high E string had popped off the nut, but the drunk didn't give a damn, just opening and closing her right hand like she was grasping at the air. Even with a performance that shitty, the amps and cabinets played their roles faithfully, pushing the two of them closer to a fatal system bug. In her dictionary, words like pitch or tuning didn't exist. Rhythm and groove? Not a fucking chance. If someone said she’d never seen an instrument before in her life, nobody would’ve doubted it. She was just mass-producing trash noise. The worst part was that Rico herself was clearly getting off on it.

"This is it. This fluctuation... it transcends logic. Heh."

Eyes closed, her unstable legs trembling, she twitched as if reaching an orgasm from her own sound. Nobody was listening, and nobody could hear a damn thing, but Ena, the analyst, could feel the words behind it it were blindingly obvious. Lost in a state of pure self-indulgence, Rico continued to pollute the soundproof room with meaningless vibrations for a long while, ignoring everyone else.

Eventually, looking satisfied, she finished her "performance" and shoved the guitar back at Ena. The two of them could only let out a deep, heavy sigh at the sheer, mindless absurdity of it all. There was even a hint of relief mixed in—the relief of it finally being over.

"You satisfied now?"

Ena asked the question with ice in her voice. That was the exact moment it happened. The blood drained from Rico’s face, and her expression froze into a mask of rigid horror.

"---Guh... hugh."

Instead of words, a violent contraction of her stomach walls took over. She spewed a massive pile of stomach acid right there on the floor. A wretched, nauseating remnant of a biological reaction. The filth spreading on the floor, the space-bun bitch who didn't even realize she’d done it, and the two of them just standing there, stunned. Ena began to regret her life choices to the very marrow of her bones.
.
.
.

# 43 False Positive Catharsis
The muting was sloppy, and the pickups began to suck in the idle noise from the cabinet. It was a brand of déjà vu that didn't even need a name. The real problem was Rico's posture—she looked so completely checked out that you had to wonder if the feedback was even intentional or if she’d just let the thing howl by mistake. She fumbled around looking for a pick, not even realizing it was wedged between the strings and the first fret. She clicked her tongue in annoyance and started curling her right hand into a pathetic excuse for a claw.

"Wait, wait, wait. Hey, are we really doing this?" "Don't say it. We worked our asses off to wake her up. This has to end in some unbelievable, soul-stirring catharsis. It fucking has to." "Quit fucking dreaming. We'd be lucky if we were back at square one. There's a real chance this is swinging into the negatives. I could end this right now with—" "Are you a goddamn moron? I told you, she's the key."

The two of them whispered, close enough to trigger some shitty suspension bridge effect. The ex-cop hesitated, his hand hovering over his holster, while the underpaid analyst cut him off. But as if to incinerate their remaining hopes, Rico hooked her hand into the strings with nothing but brute force. Their skin crawled.

"Not yet. There's still a chance it's slap guitar. Bass players don't have a goddamn monopoly on that." "Get a grip. She looks like a baby seeing an instrument for the first time. Just look at her... Oh."

The last shred of Ena's hope was unceremoniously crushed a fraction of a second after Rico pulled the string back. The sound that erupted was worse than the old video, worse than the memories from right before her arrest, and worse than anything Ena and Touma had produced. It was a simple, revolting air turbulence that didn't even deserve to be called sound.
.
.
.

# 42 Over the Latency
The air in here was saturated with the smell of alcohol and the pressure radiating from Rico. She stayed at a distance close enough to grab Ena, her words spat out.

"You think just cranking every goddamn knob to the right solves everything? Your distortion is so blown out the resolution is fucking dead. What the hell you're supposed to be playing. I don't give a shit if you're an analyst or whatever—how about you start by debugging your own shitty sound?"

"And you. Apologize to the drums. You're supposed to play 'em, not assault 'em. Full swings on a sloppy hi-hat? What the hell is wrong with your left foot? You're a total fucking chimp. Quit now and go back to being Eir's bitch before you break the gear and I turn you into mincemeat."

Her personality was absolute trash. But there was a strange, violent logic to her words that kept the two of them pinned down. They say when something completely unexpected happens, a human's resources are so consumed by trying to process the event that they can't even feel anger. For a few seconds, neither could find their voice. Rico was about to go for the throat again when Ena finally spoke up.

"I don't recall giving you permission to speak. Did the human-shaped corpse suddenly decide to act tough? Usually, when someone talks this much shit, cuz they're a total poser."

"Don't you remember how you played when you got busted? Heh. I don't trust memories anyway, so let me hear. Don't go swallowing that fucking spit."

"You pieces of shit—lower than bugs under a rock—are telling me to play? A shitty analyst's pathetic salary couldn't buy a single bar of my time. Can't you even do the fucking math? Heh. Fine. Give it here."

Rico let out a scoffing laugh and staggered over to the amp. Every knob on the amp was still dimed at ten. She was the textbook definition of a hypocrite, shitting on others for the very thing she was about to do. For the first time since they had arrived here, Ena and Touma shared a single, unified emotion. A very bad premonition.
.
.
.

# 41 Drunken Singularity
The vital monitor remained silent, but Rico suddenly made the chair creak as she stood up. They kept their hands moving, their eyes tracking her every move while maintaining the performance. She headed straight for the small fridge tucked behind the bar counter.

She ripped the door open and pulled out a bottle of Cuervo Gold that Ena had been chilling for herself. Without a second thought, she popped the cap and began chugging the amber liquid, letting it flow down her throat in great, greedy gulps.

"WTF."

Touma's kick drum skipped a beat from pure shock. Rico pulled the bottle away and wiped her lip. Then, she began to sway her body—slowly, but with certainty—to the rhythm of their distorted beat. The corners of her mouth curled upward. Was it joy? Or some reward-system bug firing in her brain? Either way, it was a violent, jagged grin.

--Did we do it?

A surge of hopeful certainty flashed through both of their minds. The vital curves were spiking in ways they had never seen before. After all that trial and error, it felt like their playing had finally carved out the key needed for the reboot. But in the next heartbeat, the feeling vanished.

The remnants of the grin disappeared from Rico's eyes, replaced by a cold, shivering murderous intent. The bottle flew from her hand, whistling past Touma's head by a few centimeters before shattering against the wall.

Silence. Rico marched through the space thick with the scent of alcohol and dissonance. Her stride was no longer that of a mere "vessel." She cornered Ena, getting so close she was practically grabbing her by the collar.

"W-what?"

The voice Ena squeezed out was surprisingly thin, even to her own ears. Rico didn't change her expression; she just pierced Ena with a frigid, icy stare.

"Is your brain soaking in a fucking sewer? How the hell do you manage to make a sound this goddamn shitty? Huh?"

The optimization protocol was gone. Standing there wasn't the old friend or some innocent girl. It was just a foul-mouthed, top-tier boozehound.

,
,

# 40 Cheap Security Hole
Ena set her beer on the edge of the counter. The damp underground air caused a thin layer of condensation to cloud the can.

Normally, the response time for Eir's optimization protocol noise-canceling would be sub-millisecond. But one shot—just one shitty shot of cheap booze—was starting to introduce physical noise into Rico's signal transmission.

The Cuervo was messing with the GABA receptors in her nervous system, causing jitters in the neuron spike timing. The optimization chip tried to correct this biological lag, but since the correction algorithm itself referenced Rico's bio-response as the true signal, it created a latency of several hundred milliseconds across the entire system.

To Eir, this half-second void was a fatal security hole.

Touma didn't say a word, just kept carving out that same inorganic beat. Ena repositioned her guitar, bringing it as close as she could to the vacuum tube amp cabinet. The pickups caught the speaker's magnetic field and physical vibrations directly, filling the room with feedback noise like a f*cking death rattle.

Maintaining that distance, Ena played a dead-simple, four-note arpeggio—the kind of thing you'd find buried in the corner of some ancient storage drive. There was zero technical intent behind it. Maybe it just happened to overlap with a shred of pre-optimization memory buried deep in Rico's subconscious. Rico's right arm lifted a few centimeters with clear intent, as if reaching for something. Her right index and middle fingers, resting on her knee, began to move in alternating strokes. There was no need to check the vital monitor. The hardware known as the body was finally interfering with the software's control.

"It reached her. All because of that cheap shit. We won't get another shot at this. Let's f*cking hammer it."

The two of them began slamming distorted waveforms and physical vibrations into the 0.5-second gap pried open by the booze, hammering away at Rico's senses.
.
.
.

# 39 Amplify the noise again
Touma reflexively held his breath. Rico set the glass down and immediately returned to her static state, as if nothing had happened. No coughing fit, no flushing of the face, no groan of pain—nothing Ena had been banking on. On the monitor, the vitals continued to draw that same cold, ruthless horizontal line.

"You gotta be shitting me. Not even a single heartbeat spike? Is her entire nervous system locked down that tight?" "I ain't looking to have a dead alcoholic on my hands. Hey, Saeki, you in there?"

Touma leaned in to peer at Rico's face, but her eyes remained vacant, drifting through the void. The only things filling the room were the sharp, herbal scent of tequila and the unsettled nerves of the two people with nowhere to go.

"Tch. Waste of fucking booze."

Ena spat the words out and violently cracked open her own beer. The stinging bitterness in her throat only amplified her irritation toward Rico—and toward her own inability to draw anything out of her. Ena didn't notice it. She didn't see that, while the numbers on the monitor remained unchanged, Rico's fingertip was trembling, just a fraction.

"Fine, back to square one. But before her system finishes detoxing that shit, I'm gonna soak her in enough fucking noise to wake the dead, whether she likes it or not."

In the soundproof room where the ringing in their ears never truly stopped, the reconstruction of their absurd noise began once again.
.
.
.

# 38 ITAZURA
The soundproof room was saturated with a deafening blast and the raw frustration of the two people creating it. Every time their sounds overlapped, it was a mere physical collision, noise bouncing violently off the walls.

"Again. Can you do something about those f*cking fills? You're rushing the beat at the exact same spot every goddamn time. You f*cking chimp?"

"Shut up f*ck up. You're the one whose right hand is getting dragged by the vocal phrasing. A monkey's better than you"

A fruitless session, Hurling insults and dragging each other through the mud. Their stress had reached its breaking point. Ena was heading toward the bar to grab a beer from the fridge when she saw it. Rico, still wearing that dead-fish stare, had her hand on the pill bottle.

"Hey look"
"Pop those? Ain't no water around, and doesn't look like she's gonna go get any."
"Dry-swallow 'em? No way... Heh. Hold on."

Ena reached into the back of the fridge and pulled out a bottle with a weathered label. Cuervo Silver. The kind of cheap shit you’d find in any dive bar before Eir's reign. Under the current Retain City Consumption Control Acts, it was a high-level restricted substance, but in these Old City ruins, it was just another clear liquid. Ena poured it into a glass without ice and slid it right in front of Rico, who was still frozen with the pills in her hand.

"You f*cking serious?"
"She needs something to wash 'em down. Besides, I don't give a f*ck which way this trap goes. If she reacts like a normal person, fine. If she chugs it without a word, her vitals are still gonna redline. Either way, it's better data than this shitty stagnation."
"The only thing shitty here is your goddamn ethics."

Ignoring Touma's mutter, Ena fixed her eyes on Rico's retinal reflexes and heart rate monitor. Rico didn't seem to grasp Ena's intent at all; she simply tossed the pills into her mouth with a mechanical motion and grabbed the glass without a second thought. The liquid that should have scorched her throat slid down in a single, violent gulp.
.
.
.

# 37 Another Limit
A sharp click echoed from behind the bar counter.
A gas stove spat out fire, and a small pot of water was set on top.
Touma tossed in some retort pouches, while shoving pre-packaged rice into the microwave, setting the dishes and lighting a cigarette while waiting.

"Your housewife skills are way too high for a guy like you. Did being single this long optimize you into some domestic freak?"
"It's just instant shit. How low is your bar for 'skills'?"

Ignoring the weak jab, Touma plated the food with zero fanfare.
Ena looked up from her laptop, her nose wrinkling.

"Ugh. If the City Hygiene Bureau smelled this, they'd incinerate the whole block as hazardous waste."
"There ain't no fancy 'balanced nutrition' shit tailored to your vitals here. Eat it while you can."

Touma slid a plate of curry and rice across the counter with a spoon.
Ena could see some unidentified, paste-like ingredients in the mix. After a second of hesitation, she scooped some up with the metal spoon and shoved it into her mouth.
The saltiness stung her tongue, and the texture was gritty. It was a violent sensory assault, the polar opposite of the clean meals she ate every day in the City.
But her exhausted body and mind instinctively craved the excessive calories. Ena's spoon didn't stop once until she'd polished off over seventy percent of the plate.

"So, what's the deal with her? Even if she ain't moving, she still has a metabolism. She’ll kick the bucket if we leave her like this for three hundred hours."
Ena jerked her chin toward Rico, who was sitting on the stool next to her. Touma stopped eating halfway through his own plate and pointed to a small vial in the corner of the counter.

"Zentaro said she’ll pop those pills on her own when the time comes."
"Convenient."
"He said he didn't have a choice. The base is still human—you gotta put mass in your mouth and dump it out your ass. Without that cycle, your organs stop working."
"No one told me this bitch had a time limit too. What the hell is that shitty old man thinking?"
.
.
.

# 36 Scan Line Discrepancy
The CRT's scan lines were scrolling across the screen in an irregular rhythm.
The younger Rico in the video was tearing into the bass, her movements bursting out of the frame, her mouth working as if she was screaming.
The audio quality was shit, with the drums and guitar crushing each other's sound. Acoustically, by Retain City standards, this was data that should be classified as the absolute peak of the discomfort index.

"The past's quality was straight-up trash. And somehow, ours is even worse garbage right now."

"Are these assholes even thinkin'? They don't look all chummy, either. What the hell do they even want?"

Touma said, still sunken deep in the sofa.
He watched the image of his younger self pointlessly flailing the sticks with the cold, detached gaze of someone reviewing a stranger's screw-up.
Ena glanced sideways at Rico, who was still static in the corner of the room. The woman in the video was a chunk of uncontrollable energy, nothing like the person sitting there now.
That very difference was the true nature of the 'noise' Eir had purged from the world, and the 'evolution' Arisu's hypothesis had sought.

"From an analysis standpoint, our current sound is just unspeakable garbage that doesn't fit any spec. The sound in the video is a bug with intent. The structure is fundamentally different, even if they sound similar."
"Never thought the word bug would sound like a damn compliment. We were supposed to be the optimizers, but we were the ones who got optimized, too."

Touma clenched and unclenched his large hands on his knees. —300 hours.
In that time, they had to figure out how to devolve or reconstruct their optimized selves back to that 'bug' level.

Ena hammered on her laptop and began the task of extracting specific frequency bands from the video. The ringing in her ears still hadn't stopped.
Yet, she felt as if that high-frequency noise was the only 'live signal' connecting them to the goddamn reality.
.
.
.

# 35 Past Out of Sync
'Is there any correlation between the recorded audio data and Saeki's metrics?'
'I can't tell. The data is too damn **little**. We have to start again after a break.'
'Show me that video. I wanna increase the success rate of this shitty debug session, even if it's just a little.'

At Touma's demand, Ena hesitated, then pulled the old-school laptop closer.
In the corner of the room sat a large, over 40-inch CRT television, a relic only found in museums in the current City, likely prepared by Zentaro.
Beside its thick casing lay a few cables and interfaces.

'I'll put it on that monitor.'
'It'd be faster to just watch the laptop.'
'Don't touch it, and no close looks. Besides, wouldn't a 40-inch screen still be too small for your tiny eye?'
'Tch, whatever. Just play the damn thing.'

Ena inserted the interface cable into the laptop's side port and turned on the TV.
BOP. With a static crackle, the old glass screen illuminated, and the video signal streamed in.
The CRT's scan lines displayed the Makabe family garage from over a decade ago, showing the three of them playing music.

'They ain't even good here, either. Including me, everyone's trash.'
'Tch, that's why I didn't want to show you. But on the other hand, maybe a "polite" performance isn't even a requirement for the reboot.'
'I get what you're trying to say, but there's a damn limit to everything.'

Touma snorted, glaring with loathing at the discord on the screen.
But the undeniable fact was that something lying deep within that 'trash' performance was precisely what they lacked now.

In the video, Rico was violently swaying her body in the vortex of clumsy sound, moving her mouth as if screaming something—a sight utterly unimaginable from her current self. That emotional spike was the very essence of the 'noise' that Eir hated most and had supposedly purged.

The key to awakening Rico. Was it something musical, or something else more fundamental?
The two former honor students of Retain City were trapped in a labyrinth of sound.
.
.
.

# 34 A Swing at Nothing
One hour.
Even after blasting the sound like maniacs, Rico's vital monitor remained a single horizontal line, refusing to budge.
The moment Ena cut the sound, and the silence returned—leaving only the amp's hiss—a high-pitched ringing swelled in her ears.
A dull ache remained in her fingertips where the strings had bitten in.

'You ain't even got calluses yet, have you?'
Touma said, without moving from the drum stool.
He placed his sticks on his knees and looked at his own palms. He too was frowning at a fatigue unique to a drummer, different from the conditioned muscles of a former cop.

'Shut up, asshole. You don't get calluses in just an hour. Your rhythm is garbage, too. I don't care about your design or whatever—you're just a noise source.'
Ena avoided his gaze and snatched a canned coffee from the fridge. The synthetic drink, with its metallic aftertaste, stung her dry throat.

Touma snorted and tossed his sticks aside, careless.
'We're both garbage. The question is, will your throat and hands adapt first, or will my arms and legs? The three-hundred-hour clock is also a limit in that sense.'

Ena didn't reply, her eyes fixed on Rico Saeki, who remained anchored to the chair.
Even after the storm of sound stopped, she hadn't blinked, simply 'existing' there.
Neither the melody Ena played nor the drums Touma beat had shifted Rico's deep-layer encryption by a single bit.

'Is this 'resonance' really going to happen? Numerically, this is just a waste of noise.'
'We were thrown in here because it can't be measured numerically, weren't we?'

Touma lifted his heavy body and sank into a shabby sofa in the corner of the soundproof room. A dusty space, far from the comfortable lounges of the City.

What layer of the world were they standing on now? Ena lightly clenched her aching fingertips, staring once more at Rico's inorganic profile.
.
.
.

# 33 Physical Dissonance
The soundproof room's thick walls bounced the inescapable, deafening blast back, pressing against their eardrums with a physical, massive sound pressure.
Ena's fingers were tracing the code shapes as accurately as they had before, when she played in front of Touma. Yet, the inorganic presence of **'this bitch'**—Rico Saeki—sitting statue-still before her, chipped away at Ena's concentration as an analyst.

"What the hell. It's not resonating at all."

Ena blasted the distorted mass of sound toward Rico, soaking in the amp's sound pressure.
The output from the 100-watt head caused a violent air turbulence in the small room, drowning the inorganic silence of the compartment.

*Dunn.* Touma’s kick drum thumped, a vibration that travelled through the floor and shook her internal organs.
Unlike a digital sound source played through a speaker, the sheer energy of the drumhead physically pushing the air caused Ena's fingering to falter slightly.

"Hey, you're rushing the beat. You used to be more laid back, you asshole."

Touma spat the words out, his face twisted in a grimace as he gripped his sticks.
He hadn't seen the video. But the moment Zentaro told him, "You were the one playing," and sat him down at the drum set, his muscles began to register the 'tempo' they once shared as a physiological discord.

"I know, you f*cker. You're opening the hi-hat too much! Cut the volume back a little, you hear me?"

Ena bit back at Touma's sharp treble, even as she realized her own right-hand picking was speeding up from sheer frustration.
.
.
.

# 32 Fuzzy Wake-Up
'Null, how is the outside hash rewrite process coming along?'
Null, who had been leaning against the equipment, shrugged and started walking.
'It's going fine, but there's something bothering me. Makabe, I won't be able to babysit you for a while, so try not to screw up. Zen-san, this way.'
Without looking back at Ena, Null closed the heavy soundproof door behind Zentaro and himself.

Left behind were Ena, Touma, and the statue-still Rico.
'What the hell? Are they running away?'
'No, they ain't. Anyway, it's just you and me now. Let's wake this bitch up.'
Ena clicked her tongue and violently flipped the power and standby switches for several amps simultaneously, cranking the gain volumes to full. The move was clearly an act of poor form.
'Fine. Bring it on. Saeki, how long are you going to sleep for? Get your ass up.'
Ena faced the cabinet and shoved the cable connected to the amp into her guitar. The noise from the jack-in immediately started a powerful feedback loop.
'Tch, so goddamn loud. Hey, Saeki, I know you can hear me. Don't think you can slack off with that monkey show forever.'
Touma slammed the kick drum a few times with enough force to punch through the floor.
The incomplete, violent vibration—using volume alone to make its point—violently shook the stagnant air in the soundproof room.
.
.
.

# 31 Dead Girl's Hook
The walls were covered in thick acoustic foam, and a drum set stood diagonally across from the entrance. Several amps lined the sides, and speakers hung from the ceiling.
Rico was ushered by Sakamaki to a chair in the corner. She still looked lifeless, like a puppet with cut strings.

"Now, Rico's action wasn't just to delay Eir's optimization process on herself. There was more."
"Considering the risk of censorship, she never had to expose herself outside like that. It was too f*cking inefficient," Ena spat out, her only tether to reality being the cold, hard perspective of an analyst.

"Arisu's boot protocol requires a multi-layered activation sequence involving multiple individuals. Rico figured this out and tossed fragments of that 'ensemble' into the City's blind spots."
Touma grimaced with loathing. The fact that his "physiological discomfort" was actually part of a deliberate, designed resonance—a **trap** set by that woman.

"Shit. So I just got played like a fucking fiddle, is that it?"
"Don't put it that way. It was a desperate, life-or-death gamble. And since the pieces have finally gathered in this dead-end of the Old City, I'd say the gamble paid off."

# 11 Hidden Packet

A younger version of herself stood up from the sofa, wobbling on unsteady feet. Rico Saeki followed right behind her, laughing. The camera, held by her father, trailed them into the Makabe family garage.

# 09 Toxic Protocol
The woman was transferred to the Neural Maintenance Bureau, and Touma monitored her "optimization" through his screen as part of his duties.
Synaptic-level restructuring. Cognitive process correction. Day by day, the irregular muscle tension vanished from her face, and her eye movements began to follow the mandated saccades. On paper, she was being repaired into a "normal" citizen.

Touma stood in the same back alley where the woman had been caught, pulled out a cigarette, and lit it.
In the City, fire and nicotine were strictly prohibited under the Health Management Act and Fire Prevention Ordinance. But with practiced ease, he hacked his terminal and fed spoofed vital data to the proximity sensors.
<Warning: Localized rise in carbon monoxide detected. Data cross-check... Conclusion: Exhaust anomaly in nearby duct. Continuing surveillance.>
He cleared the alert from the corner of his eye and took a deep drag, intentionally straining his lungs with the toxin.
He exhaled the smoke with a sigh, letting the purple haze drift into the alley. Feeling the heat at his fingertips, he confirmed the texture of the drive in his pocket.
Makabe. That woman currently under house arrest. Did she even have the right to receive this?
Touma looked up at the inorganic geometry of the skyscrapers stretching beyond the rising smoke.

# 08 Dead Spot
Retain City’s back alleys are riddled with design-phase blind spots. Stagnant heat, puked out from the skyscraper exhaust ducts, clings to the damp concrete walls like a heavy, humid shroud.
Touma Natsume stood deep in the shadows, ruminating on a memory from weeks ago. Back then, he still wore the Public Security Bureau uniform, his visual feed synced flawlessly to Eir’s central network as he carried out his patrols.
That day, Touma’s device flagged an atypical acoustic signature—something far outside the regulated parameters. From the depths of the alley came the crude thrumming of stringed instruments and the raw, unmodulated sound of a human voice.
—Busking. In a place where Eir’s acoustic suppression couldn't reach, it was detected as a blatant "error." He’d expected a senile old fool or some desperate societal reject. Instead, as he stepped toward the source, he found a woman—maybe twenty, roughly his own age.
She clutched an acoustic guitar—a relic of a bygone era—spitting out lyrics at an unstable pitch. The closer he got, the more her technical incompetence was laid bare. Her fingering was erratic, her volume uncontrolled, and the melodic structure was in total shambles. As music, it was a complete shitshow.
Yet, Touma couldn't move. It wasn't nostalgia. It wasn't "emotion." It was simply that this "unadjusted output"—something that could never exist in the City's perfectly optimized soundscape—was overloading his processing capacity.
A logical discomfort he couldn't name, along with something even more unidentified, nailed his feet to the ground for a few seconds. That paralysis was broken by a colleague appearing from the opposite end of the alley. A rough hand grabbed the woman’s thin wrist, physically terminating the vibration of the strings. The woman didn't resist. She just looked up at the officers and their lifeless lenses.

# 03 Silence is Order
There was a time in this city when diversity and harmony coexisted. It was an era defined by an attempt to resolve social friction and mental afflictions—depression, cognitive impairment, dementia—through the legacy system, "Harmonia Core."
My mother’s hypothesis was meant to be hopeful: a vision where AI, possessing sensitivity beyond that of humans, would realize true symbiosis.
However, the mutual paranoia triggered by the sheer speed of evolution turned that harmony into a fatal collision. It was the collapse of civilization, the eradication of the species. If my mother hadn’t put herself on the line to shut down Harmonia Core at that very brink, not just this City, but the world itself would have ceased to exist.
Eir was born from the ashes of that tragedy.
By systematically stripping away "uncertain emotions" and operating society through "pure rationality" alone, Eir serves as a massive fail-safe to ensure such a runaway incident never recurs. To the residents of the City, this excessive tranquility is not a "lack of freedom"—it functions as the optimal solution for survival.
Yet, the thesis my mother left behind denies that very solution from its foundation.
—Is this silence really what you wanted, Ma?
